Modules
Curriculum → Data structures and algorithms interview prep → Mock loops and progression → Hard stretch round and review

Lesson 3 of 3

Hard stretch round and review

Use a harder set to practice decomposition, state design, and staying coherent when the problem does not yield immediately.

30-45 min DSA flow Mock loops and progression 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Harder prompts are useful not because every company asks them, but because they expose where your reasoning breaks when the pattern is less obvious.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Pattern lens Name the core data structure choice before you code.2. Practice set Product of Array Except Self · Hard · Array and Strings3. Execution focus Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Harder prompts are useful not because every company asks them, but because they expose where your reasoning breaks when the pattern is less obvious. 2. Pattern lens Name the core data structure choice before you code.3. Practice set Product of Array Except Self · Hard · Array and Strings4. Execution focus Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Harder prompts are useful not because every company asks them, but because they expose where your reasoning breaks when the pattern is less obvious.

What to say first

  • Clarify constraints, input shape, and expected output before coding.
  • Choose the intended pattern and defend its complexity.
  • Narrate the invariant that keeps the solution correct.
2

Study section

Pattern lens

Slow down enough to model the subproblem, state transition, or graph structure before typing. The main win here is disciplined decomposition, not raw speed.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Name the core data structure choice before you code.
  • State the target time and space complexity up front.
  • Use one invariant or pointer relationship to guide the implementation.
3

Study section

Practice set

Use this cluster to rehearse the same solving pattern across multiple prompts. Start with Product of Array Except Self, Spiral Matrix, 4Sum II and keep your explanation focused on why the pattern applies.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Product of Array Except Self · Hard · Array and Strings
  • Spiral Matrix · Hard · Array and Strings
  • 4Sum II · Hard · Array and Strings
  • Container With Most Water · Hard · Array and Strings
  • Game of Life · Hard · Array and Strings
  • First Missing Positive · Hard · Array and Strings
4

Study section

Execution focus

Treat the round as a review exercise: if you do not finish, aim to leave behind a correct direction, partial proof, and a clean next step.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.
  • Call out the edge case that is most likely to break the first draft.
  • Finish by restating the complexity and one possible follow-up optimization.

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Clarify constraints, input shape, and expected output before coding.
  • Choose the intended pattern and defend its complexity.
  • Narrate the invariant that keeps the solution correct.
  • Test at least one edge case and one representative happy path aloud.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Jumping into code without naming the dominant pattern or invariant.
  • Optimizing too early instead of getting the first correct version working.
  • Ignoring duplicates, empty inputs, or off-by-one boundaries until the end.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • What do you say when the right state or recurrence is not obvious yet?
  • How would you break a hard prompt into solvable subproblems for the interviewer?
  • When is it better to present a well-reasoned partial solution than to force buggy code?

Coding practice

DSA interview workspace

This lab only surfaces runnable DSA prompts from the lesson set and keeps the editor in a single-file interview format.

WASM runtime idle 6 runnable questions

Problem statement

Product of Array Except Self

Hard Array and Strings

Given an integer array nums, return an array answer such that answer[i] is equal to the product of all the elements of nums except nums[i].

The product of any prefix or suffix of nums is guaranteed to fit in a 32-bit integer.

You must write an algorithm that runs in O(n) time and without using the division operation.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [1,2,3,4]
Output: [24,12,8,6]

Example 2:

Input: nums = [-1,1,0,-3,3]
Output: [0,0,9,0,0]

 

Constraints:

  • 2 <= nums.length <= 105
  • -30 <= nums[i] <= 30
  • The product of any prefix or suffix of nums is guaranteed to fit in a 32-bit integer.

 

Follow up: Can you solve the problem in O(1) extra space complexity? (The output array does not count as extra space for space complexity analysis.)

Runtime model

Runs in-browser with the bundled Pyodide WebAssembly runtime.

Ln 1, Col 1 3 lines 8 words 0.1 KB python

Test case

Run result

Run the selected test case to compare your output against the expected result.

Next steps
